prior.title: UK Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs: Organizational Overview and Policy Framework
prior.summary: This is the primary landing page for the UK Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs (Defra), a ministerial department responsible for environmental protection, green economy development, and rural community sustainability. The page provides an overview of Defra's 34 supporting agencies and public bodies, recent policy announcements including water system overhaul, species recovery funding, golden eagle reintroduction, and trail hunting ban, along with guidance on import regulations, pet travel, and agricultural certifications.
prior.full_brief:
I'll synthesise across all provided source blocks to produce a structured briefing on DEFRA's organisational overview and policy framework, anchoring every claim to a verbatim source span from the supplied URLs.

---

# UK Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs: Organisational Overview and Policy Framework

## Market Signal Brief

---

# 1. What's Moving and What Triggered It

MONITORING

DEFRA is a ministerial department of the Government of the United Kingdom, responsible for environmental protection, food production and standards, agriculture, fisheries, and rural communities in England. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

The department was formed in June 2001 under the leadership of Margaret Beckett, when the Ministry of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food (MAFF) was merged with part of the Department of Environment, Transport and the Regions (DETR) and with a small part of the Home Office. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

DEFRA's overarching aim is sustainable development, which is defined as "development which enables all people throughout the world to satisfy their basic needs and enjoy a better quality of life without compromising the quality of life of future generations." *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

Under this overarching aim, DEFRA operates five strategic priorities: (1) Climate change and energy; (2) Sustainable consumption and production, including responsibility for the National Waste Strategy; (3) Protecting the countryside and natural resource protection; (4) Sustainable rural communities; (5) A sustainable farming and food sector including animal health and welfare. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

DEFRA leads for the United Kingdom on agricultural, fisheries, and environmental matters in international negotiations on sustainable development and climate change. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating global freight forwarding across UK trade lanes — particularly those handling air-priority cargo into or out of the UK — DEFRA's simultaneous stewardship of waste policy (National Waste Strategy), environmental targets, biosecurity and circular economy regulation constitutes the single most consequential UK domestic policy environment. Monitoring DEFRA's five strategic priorities is therefore not background context; it is tracking the legislative pipeline for packaging, waste, biosecurity, and trade-adjacent environmental obligations in the UK market.

---

# 2. Who's Driving It and What They Want

The Secretary of State responsible is Emma Reynolds MP. The Permanent Secretary is Paul Kissack. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The DEFRA Board provides strategic, corporate leadership to the department and has particular responsibility for monitoring performance and delivery; it meets once a quarter. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

Board membership includes ministers, senior officials, and non-executives. The ministerial members are: Emma Reynolds MP (Secretary of State), Dame Angela Eagle DBE MP (Minister of State, Minister for Food Security and Rural Affairs), Emma Hardy MP (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State, Minister for Water and Flooding), Mary Creagh CBE MP (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State, Minister for Nature), and Baroness Hayman of Ullock (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State, Lords Minister). *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The senior official members are Paul Kissack (Permanent Secretary) and Iain King (Chief Financial Officer and Interim Director General for Delivery and Group Chief Operating Officer). Non-executive members are Heather Hancock (Lead Non-Executive Board Member), Nick Folland, Indro Mukerjee, Sachin Jogia, and Dan Corry. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The Executive Committee (ExCo) is a sub-committee of the board and is the senior decision-making body for the core department. It is responsible for building a culture that supports integrated ways of working that will deliver the group's strategic objectives, and ensures strategic allocation and management of resources across the DEFRA group to align with priorities. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

ExCo members are: Paul Kissack (Permanent Secretary), Emily Miles (Director General for Food, Farming and Biosecurity), David Hill (Director General for Strategy and Water), Sally Randall (Director General for Environment), Anjali Goswami (Chief Scientific Adviser and Director General for Science, Data and Analysis), Emma Bourne (Director General for EU Reset and Trade), Iain King (CFO and Interim DG for Delivery and Group COO), Nicola Bettesworth (Group HR Director), and Christian Cubitt (Group Communications Director). *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The Delivery Committee is a sub-committee of the DEFRA Board. It encourages focus on outcomes, helps understand how to achieve goals, and oversees DEFRA Group operational performance where that activity is important for achieving outcomes. It monitors and manages significant risks and makes strategic choices about priorities, sequencing, and pace. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The DEFRA Audit and Risk Assurance Committee (ARAC) is a sub-committee of the DEFRA Board and is an advisory body with no executive powers or responsibilities. It supports and advises the DEFRA Board and the Principal Accounting Officer (the Permanent Secretary) on issues of risk, control and governance. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

The Investment Committee is a sub-committee of the Executive Committee. It assures and approves Tier 1 and Tier 2 project business cases, specifically focusing on those which breach delegated authority limits, and provides approvals for all consultancy spend over £100,000. *Source: DEFRA Board Governance Page, GOV.UK, accessed June 2026. https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/department-for-environment-food-rural-affairs/about/our-governance.

**Ministerial portfolio distribution relevant to freight and logistics workspaces:**

Mary Creagh CBE MP (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Nature) holds the portfolio for circular economy; planning and land use framework; domestic biodiversity; tree planting and forestry; international nature and wildlife; environmental targets and EIP; green finance; and Protected Landscapes. She leads for the Office for Environmental Protection, Natural England, Forestry Commission, Joint Nature Conservation Committee, and Kew Gardens. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

Emma Hardy MP (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Water and Flooding) holds the portfolio for floods and emergencies; water; clean air and noise; environmental regulation (including chemicals, contaminated land and REACH); pesticides; Net Zero, climate change and adaptation; domestic and international marine. She leads for the Environment Agency and Consumer Council for Water. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

Baroness Hayman (Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Biosecurity, Borders and Animals) holds the portfolio for biosecurity and plant health; borders; Northern Ireland – Windsor Framework; animal welfare; One Health; access (including rights of way and coastal paths). She leads for the Veterinary Medicines Directorate and Animal and Plant Health Agency. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

*Analytical inference:* The concentration of circular economy, packaging, waste strategy, REACH-equivalent chemicals, clean air, biosecurity, and border-adjacent environmental regulation within a single departmental structure means that DEFRA produces regulatory outputs that intersect freight operations across multiple compliance dimensions simultaneously. Workspaces handling goods subject to UK environmental product requirements, packaging obligations, or biosecurity border checks interact with DEFRA's regulatory pipeline whether or not they transact directly with the department.

---

# 3. Expected Trajectory and Conversion Triggers

The Environment, Food and Rural Affairs Committee (EFRA) is the Commons Select Committee that scrutinises the work of DEFRA. It looks at issues from farming to the water sector to a circular economy. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

As of June 2026, the EFRA Committee has nine current inquiries. Active inquiries with direct freight and logistics relevance include:

- **Preventing waste and enabling a circular economy** — opened 20 May 2025, currently accepting written submissions (open for submissions). *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Land use and nature** — opened 18 March 2026, currently accepting written evidence (open for submissions). *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Climate adaptation and emergency response** — report published 20 March 2026; government response overdue since 20 May 2026; opened 9 September 2025. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Fisheries and the marine environment** — report published 24 April 2026; government response due by 24 June 2026; opened 23 January 2025. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Biosecurity and animal welfare** — report published 5 June 2026; government response due by 5 August 2026; opened 9 January 2025. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Food supply chain resilience and fairness** — opened 20 December 2024, ongoing. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Reforming the water sector** — report published 1 May 2026; government response due by 1 July 2026; opened 19 December 2024. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **The future of farming** — report published 16 May 2025; government response published 3 September 2025; opened 6 December 2024. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

- **Work of the Department and its arm's-length bodies** — report published 29 April 2026; opened 6 November 2024. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.

*Analytical inference:* The simultaneous publication of reports on biosecurity, fisheries, water, and departmental performance — all with government responses due or overdue within the July–August 2026 window — indicates a period of accelerated DEFRA policy response pressure. Workspaces monitoring UK circular economy and biosecurity border requirements should track government responses to the biosecurity and animal welfare report (due 5 August 2026) and the reforming the water sector report (due 1 July 2026) as conversion indicators for regulatory activity in those domains.

**Re-check window:** Pull government responses to EFRA biosecurity report (5 August 2026) and EFRA water sector report (1 July 2026) to determine whether DEFRA commitments convert to statutory or guidance instruments. Re-check this brief in September 2026.

---

# 4. Operational and Cost Implications If It Materialises

**DEFRA's arm's-length body and delivery partner structure — operational relevance to freight workspaces:**

DEFRA's executive agencies are: Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA); Centre for Environment, Fisheries and Aquaculture Science (CEFAS); Rural Payments Agency (RPA); and Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD). *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

Key delivery partners include: Agriculture and Horticulture Development Board; Consumer Council for Water; Environment Agency; Fera Science; Forestry Commission; Joint Nature Conservation Committee; Marine Management Organisation; National Forest Company; Natural England; Office for Environmental Protection; Ofwat; Royal Botanic Gardens, Kew; Sea Fish Industry Authority. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

*Analytical inference:* For workspaces in the freight forwarding role handling goods subject to UK biosecurity border requirements — including live animals, plant materials, food, and associated packaging — APHA and the VMD are the operative agencies with direct border interface. Workspaces handling ocean freight through UK ports interact with the Marine Management Organisation and CEFAS's regulatory remit on fisheries and marine environment standards. These are not peripheral contacts; they are the implementing agencies for the compliance obligations that DEFRA's policy framework generates.

**Responsibilities with direct freight and trade adjacency:**

DEFRA is responsible for UK government policy in: adaptation to global warming; agriculture; air quality; animal health and animal welfare; biodiversity conservation; chemical substances and pesticides; fisheries; flooding; food; forestry; land management; marine policy; plant health; rural development; sustainable development; trade and the environment; waste management; water management. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

Some policies apply to England alone due to devolution, while others are not devolved and therefore apply to the United Kingdom as a whole. Concordats set out agreed frameworks for cooperation between DEFRA and the Scottish Government, Welsh Government, and Northern Ireland Executive, which have devolved responsibilities for these matters in their respective nations. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

*Legal Confirmation Required:* Which specific DEFRA-originated regulatory obligations apply UK-wide versus England-only for a given cargo vertical (e.g., packaging waste, REACH-equivalent chemicals, biosecurity) is a devolution question that turns on the specific instrument. Legal confirmation is required on a regulation-by-regulation basis.

**Spending baseline:**

*Specific total DEFRA spending figure for 2024–25 not available from primary source spans in the provided source blocks as of June 2026.* The DEFRA Annual Report and Accounts 2024–25 was identified as a corroborating source and is listed under New Sources Identified; the £5.52bn spending and 31,616 FTE staffing figures attributed to that document are not reproduced verbatim in the provided source content and therefore cannot be asserted as FACT-class claims in this brief. They are flagged as research leads for direct retrieval.

---

# 5. Competitive Implications

*Analytical inference:* DEFRA's regulatory pipeline — particularly on circular economy (Preventing Waste and Enabling a Circular Economy inquiry, opened 20 May 2025), biosecurity border requirements, and environmental product standards — creates differentiated compliance burdens across freight forwarding competitors based on cargo vertical, trade lane, and supply chain role. Workspaces that track DEFRA's EFRA Committee inquiry outputs ahead of government response can anticipate regulatory instruments before competitors who wait for final published guidance.

*Analytical inference:* The EFRA Committee inquiry into "Work of the Department and its arm's-length bodies" (report published 29 April 2026) is a forward signal on DEFRA's own assessment of delivery capacity. Analytical inference: where the Committee identifies delivery gaps in ALBs, the gap typically precedes either a reform instrument or a period of reduced enforcement capacity, both of which carry operational implications for workspaces operating under those ALBs' inspection or permitting regimes.

*Analytical inference:* DEFRA's portfolio for "EU Reset and trade" (held at ExCo level by Emma Bourne as Director General) signals active engagement on UK–EU regulatory divergence, particularly in areas where post-Brexit alignment or divergence affects goods moving on the UK–EU trade lane. Workspaces operating cross-channel road and air freight should treat this DG portfolio as a forward indicator for changes to mutual recognition, border requirements, and product standard alignment on that lane.

No named competitor positions were available from the provided source blocks. Competitor-level positioning requires sourced trade reporting and is not available for assertion here.

---

# 6. Client Conversation Talking Points

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- DEFRA is the UK's primary regulatory authority for environmental protection, packaging and waste, biosecurity, and food standards. Its five strategic priorities — climate change and energy; sustainable consumption and production; natural resource protection; sustainable rural communities; and sustainable farming and food — define the UK's environmental legislative pipeline for the foreseeable future. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.
- DEFRA's circular economy inquiry (EFRA Committee, opened May 2025) is still open for submissions. For clients with packaging, waste, or product-stewardship obligations on UK lanes, the inquiry's scope — from farming to water to circular economy — means any client operating goods subject to UK packaging rules should be monitoring the output. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.
- DEFRA's biosecurity and animal welfare inquiry produced a report on 5 June 2026; the government response is due by 5 August 2026. For clients moving live animals, plant materials, or regulated food products through UK borders, this is the next formal policy signal point. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/.
- Some DEFRA policies apply to England only; others apply UK-wide. The devolution question matters on specific cargo and route combinations and should not be assumed without instrument-level review. *Source: Wikipedia – Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, Wikimedia Foundation, accessed June 2026. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Department_for_Environment,_Food_and_Rural_Affairs.

**Pitfalls to avoid:**

- Do not assert specific penalty figures, compliance deadlines, or registration obligations based on DEFRA's organisational overview alone. DEFRA produces the policy framework; specific instruments (extended producer responsibility regulations, biosecurity border statutory instruments, REACH UK secondary legislation) carry their own deadlines and obligations and require instrument-level review.
- Do not conflate England-only DEFRA policies with UK-wide obligations on client conversations without first identifying the specific instrument's devolution scope.
- Do not characterise EFRA Committee inquiry outputs as DEFRA policy commitments. Committee reports are parliamentary scrutiny outputs; they trigger a government response obligation but do not themselves create regulatory obligations.

---

# 7. What the Workspace Should Do Now

**Action 1 — Track EFRA government responses in the July–August 2026 window.**
Four EFRA Committee reports have government responses due before end of August 2026: water sector (1 July 2026), fisheries and marine environment (24 June 2026), biosecurity and animal welfare (5 August 2026), and the climate adaptation report (response overdue since 20 May 2026). *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/. Workspaces handling UK-routed freight in live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, humanitarian cargo, or any goods subject to biosecurity border requirements should assign a staff member to retrieve and scan each government response on publication date. Government responses to EFRA reports are published at committees.parliament.uk and on GOV.UK.

**Action 2 — Retrieve the DEFRA Annual Report and Accounts 2024–25 directly.**
The Annual Report and Accounts 2024–25 (https://assets.publishing.service.gov.uk/media/6914a1209d50fc2fe81616c3/Defra-ARA-2024-25-laying.pdf) carries DEFRA's stated spending, programme priorities, and ALB performance data for the period. This document was identified as a corroborating source but its content was not available in verbatim form in the provided source blocks. Direct retrieval will provide the grounded spending baseline, programme commitments, and ALB performance narrative needed for client-facing briefings on UK regulatory readiness. Add to source registry on retrieval.

**Action 3 — Retrieve the NAO DEFRA Overview 2024–25.**
The National Audit Office's independent assessment (https://www.nao.org.uk/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/defra-overview-2023-24.pdf) was identified as a corroborating source but returned no substantive content in the provided source blocks (the retrieved content was the NAO website navigation shell, not the document body). Direct retrieval will provide the independent performance assessment, budget analysis, and resilience challenge narrative that the NAO applies to DEFRA's delivery — material that is typically more candid than DEFRA's own annual report on delivery gaps and risk.

**Action 4 — Map cargo verticals to DEFRA ALB interfaces.**
Workspaces handling cargo that crosses DEFRA ALB jurisdiction — APHA for biosecurity, VMD for veterinary medicines, Environment Agency for waste and environmental permitting, Marine Management Organisation for fisheries and marine goods — should document which ALB is the operative interface for each trade lane and cargo type. This mapping supports faster response when DEFRA policy outputs convert to specific instrument-level obligations.

**Action 5 — Contribute to the EFRA circular economy inquiry if the workspace has relevant evidence.**
The "Preventing waste and enabling a circular economy" inquiry (opened 20 May 2025) is still open for submissions. *Source: UK Parliament EFRA Committee, UK Parliament, accessed June 2026. https://committees.parliament.uk/committee/52/environment-food-and-rural-affairs-committee/. *Analytical inference:* For workspaces operating reusable transport packaging, managing packaging waste obligations on UK trade lanes, or advising clients on UK circular economy compliance, a written submission to this inquiry is a low-cost mechanism to place operational evidence directly before the parliamentary body scrutinising DEFRA's waste policy development — and to monitor the scope of forthcoming regulatory proposals.

---
